tipRules.tip: KVD: 'Fall reservoir creek arms — line up with the creek channel edge and fire long casts ahead of the boat. Use the squarebill for surveillance, running down edges, hitting bottom, stumps, laydowns, docks. Work it fast and erratic. Bump everything. 17-20lb fluorocarbon to rip through grass.'

KVD: For the shallow zone in cold water, use a flatside crankbait (Hardliner). Extremely tight action — bass use their lateral line to hunt in cold water and flatsides displace a ton of water. Small profile matters in cold water.

KVD: 'Fall — start with a KVD 1.5 in a shad pattern in the backs of creeks. Bass are feeding on shad. Look for surface activity and swarms of shad along creek channel banks, sand/mud flats, rocky shorelines.'
KVD: When grass clumps reach the surface, a regular squarebill runs too deep. Switch to the 2.5 wake — choose baits that are efficient for the cover in that depth zone.

KVD: Flatside crankbait (Hardliner 35) is the FIRST crankbait in extreme cold — 'the difference between a flatside and even a squarebill is night and day in the amount of bites in extremely cold water.' Smaller profile, no rattle, but flat sides give off huge pressure waves fish home in on.

KVD: Square bills have great deflection qualities and are one of the most weedless crankbaits. The square bill kicks off hard from grass, brush, gnarly rocks, and riprap — triggering neutral or negative fish. Works cold or warm.
KVD: 'Bass can be finicky about baitfish size in fall — downsize to a KVD 1.0 squarebill. That smaller bait can be phenomenal.'

KVD: 'No place in the country I go without a 1.5 tied on in spring. It's versatile around laydowns, rocks, deeper grass. Vary line size to control depth — heavier line (up to 20lb fluoro) to run shallower, lighter line to dig deeper.'

KVD: 'When bass are dirt shallow near spawn, use the 1.5 Shallow — runs about 12 inches below surface. Throw on heavy 17lb line near shallow cover and docks. Rod tip high and it practically doubles as a wakebait. Use bluegill/sunfish colors — bass are targeting them around beds.'

KVD: 'The dream scenario for a squarebill in fall is a rocky creek channel swing in the back of a major tributary, with shad active near the surface. It's an efficient bait because bass are moving shallow and will bite it in just about any color of water.'

KVD: 'For smallmouth cranking on 5-7ft flats, use a squarebill. Key difference from largemouth: DON'T dig into the bottom. Smallmouth feed by looking up — burn the bait over their heads a few feet off bottom. Use 17lb fluorocarbon (heavier than normal since bottom contact isn't needed).'

KVD: 'Run the 1.5 or 2.5 into cover — even in clear water the erratic swimming action triggers strikes. Stop-and-start retrieve, let the bait hunt around. Use rod height to control depth — tip up to tick above grass, tip down for deeper.'
KVD: 'This has no rattle, so it's real quiet. I throw it in tough conditions when the bite is a bit off — the silent erratic action still triggers reflex strikes.'

KVD: 'Cold or dirty water: switch from standard squarebill to flat-sided crankbait (Chick Magnet). Round bill on circuit board, extremely flat sides that displace a ton of water — gives off sonic images to help bass find it in stained/muddy water. Excels in late fall, early spring, and winter. Standard 1.5 squarebill is better year-round for cover (logs, laydowns, brush, rocks).'

KVD: 'Fall dirty water after heavy rain — head farther back in creeks into the dirty water. Incoming stained water pulls shad toward current and bass follow. Fish get extremely shallow and tight to cover. That's when a squarebill shines.'

KVD: 'Power fishing the spawn: use a squarebill to locate stumps, then make persistent casts from various angles. Slow the retrieve to keep bait banging on cover for erratic deflection. Several big fish came after 4+ casts to the same stump. If snagged, snap free from a distance or break off — never go over the stump and disturb the area.'

KVD: 'A shallow diving crankbait is extremely effective for covering water in winter. Make repetitive casts down rock banks. Bass prefer a slow, tight wobble over wide hunting action in frigid temps — flat-sided crankbaits excel.'

KVD: 'Summer shallow — use a bigger squarebill (2.5 or 4.0) in bluegill patterns around spawning panfish. Both have plenty of vibration for dirty water. Conditions dictate — high dirty water means shallow bass even in summer.'

KVD: 'Pre-spawn rocky areas and channel swings: squarebill is unbeatable. Use crawfish patterns — Delta Red, Fire Craw, Brown Crawfish, Red Tomato. Position boat close to bank and make short, accurate parallel casts to keep bait in the strike zone.'

KVD: 'Shallow zone cold water cranking: flat-sided crankbait with tight wobble, small profile, no loud rattle. Fish channel swing ends, flat edges, steep-to-shallow transitions, and rock. Even in dirty water, bass locate it by lateral line.'

KVD: 'In clearer fall creek arms, switch from Sexy Shad to Summer Sexy Shad color on a 1.5 squarebill. Fish it around laydowns, windy banks, and especially shallow floating boat docks in October/November. It's the shad migration toward back of major creeks — but if water is clearer than usual, the subtler color is better.'
KVD: 'Shallow to medium running crankbaits are my fall preference. Keep your lure off the bottom — baitfish are suspended in the water column. Match lure size to baitfish size.'

presentationRules.retrieveNote: 17-20lb fluorocarbon to rip through grass.'
Smallmouth feed by looking up — burn the bait over their heads a few feet off bottom
If snagged, snap free from a distance or break off — never go over the stump and disturb the area.'

tipRules.tip: KVD: 'Red Eye Shad is at its best in cold water. It always makes bottom contact and finds inside weed edges. Ripping it free triggers strikes. It has a shimmy as it sinks like a stick worm — dynamite for yo-yoing inside edges. Sweep rod to the side, let it sink, repeat, moving bait 4-6 feet each sweep.'

KVD: Fish the Red Eye Shad like a worm or jig, not just cast-and-reel. Rip it out of cover, let it flutter down — that swimming shimmy on the fall is the magic. The colder it is, the slower you fish. Downsize to 1/4oz on ultra-shallow flats (1-2ft). Upsize line to 17-20lb fluoro to slow the fall and keep bait above grass.
KVD: 'Red Eye Shad is just dynamite when fish start moving out of the basin onto flats. That's when spring fishing gets really good.'
KVD: In stained water with high bright skies, you can still make fish react. Gold Sexy Shad is a go-to color when the water has that stain color.

KVD: In extreme cold on grass lakes, upsize line to 17-20lb fluoro to slow the bait down. Downsize to quarter or 3/8 oz for ultra-shallow flats. Fish it like a jig — short hopping, not just cast and reel. The shimmy/quiver fall is what makes the Red Eye Shad special.

KVD: 'The bait HAS to hit something — rocks, grass, bottom. Simply retrieving in a straight line won't yield results. If the bait isn't hitting anything, stop reeling and let it sink until it does. Deflection and disturbance trigger strikes.'

KVD: 'Red Eye Shad is my go-to as soon as water opens — even with ice still floating. The falling action triggers the most strikes. Fish it like a yo-yo: side sweep of rod, let it fall, repeat. The balanced shimmy as it drops looks like a wounded baitfish. Rip it off grass or stumps for reaction strikes.'
KVD: 'On shallow flats, use your rod tip and line size (10-20lb) to control depth. I want the bait hitting bottom. Every time it ticks cover, pop it and let it flutter — that's when fish bite.'

KVD: 'My favorite technique is pump-and-stop. Rod tip to the side, sweep it, follow the bait back down on a tight line. Lets the Red Eye Shad swim to bottom with its shimmer. I catch more fish this way than any other.'
KVD: 'In deeper water, yo-yo on ledges — cast, let sink, hop up and follow back down. The 3/4oz gets down 15-20 feet. Use lighter line and fluorocarbon to keep the bait deep.'

KVD: At Guntersville, bass get super shallow on river bars — a 1/4oz Red Eye Shad is 'absolutely deadly' there. Most people use 1/2oz but these fish are in 1-2ft of water. It's all about the fall — rip it out of the grass and let it flutter with that shimmy/hover.

KVD: 'Fall with 2ft visibility — Red Eye Shad 1/2oz is my absolute favorite. Vary line size to control depth: 17lb fluoro for 2ft zone, lighter for deeper. Must contact bottom or nip grass. Pumping retrieve — pull/stop/pull like a jig only faster. The unique fluttering fall triggers bites. Sexy Shad color; perch/bluegill in northern lakes.'

KVD: Red Eye Shad is one of the most efficient baits for covering flat spawning areas. In spring in Texas, red = crawfish coming out. Stained water = fire craw or brighter reds. As sun comes up, switch to gold sexy shad — great flash in stained water.

KVD: Don't just cast and reel a Red Eye Shad — yo-yo it, tick the grass, rip it out. Follow the ditches and drains on your LakeMaster. Look for subtle depressions on big flats — sometimes the best fish are on features you can only find by fishing, not on the map.

KVD: 'In stained water over grass during pre-spawn, the Red Eye Shad is a primary go-to. It covers water and has inherent reaction qualities to trigger cold-water strikes. You can alter action by jerking, ripping, or pausing to interest neutral fish.'

KVD: 'Winter lipless crankbait: pull-and-pause retrieve to keep bait falling near the bottom. Make parallel casts along structure — stay at the fish's depth the entire retrieve. Very methodical, multiple casts to an area. Bass prefer structure with vertical edges so they can move up and down as conditions change.'

KVD: Cold front makes pre-spawn fish swipe and slap at the Red Eye Shad instead of eating it. Triple grip hooks catch them foul-hooked in the back or dorsal — 'that's why you use triple grips.' Won a Classic doing exactly this.

KVD: 'In stained water over grass during prespawn, the Red Eye Shad or slow-rolled spinnerbait are my go-to power baits. They cover water and feature inherent reaction qualities to trigger cold-water strikes.'

KVD: 'Early spring cold water (40s-low 50s): 1/2oz Red Eye Shad in red/crawfish colors. Key is to fish it near the bottom — drag or short-hop it, don't burn it. Use a slower 6.1:1 reel to force a slower retrieve. Won 2010 Classic on Lay Lake with this approach — still ice on backwaters.'
KVD: 'For combing large expanses of prespawn water, I love a lipless crankbait like the Red Eye Shad. I use this faster-moving lure in the upper ranges of the prespawn temp zone (52-55°F).'

defaultTips.tip: KVD: 'To really unlock the power of a Red Eye Shad, fish it like a worm or a jig.' Rip out of grass, let it flutter. The quiver/shimmy fall triggers bites from tracking bass. Won the Classic at Lay Lake with this exact technique.

KVD: 'If you're just casting and reeling back, you're selling yourself short. The beauty of the Red Eye Shad is it stays balanced and doesn't tip over — it has this shimmer as it swims back to bottom. That's when the fish really bite it.'

tipRules.tip: KVD: 'After catching a good fish cranking, pitch back with a Texas-rigged Ocho on slack line. In clear fall water it's deadly — 1/8oz unpegged tungsten, 14lb fluorocarbon, 7ft baitcaster. Let it sink to bottom even in 20 feet. Fish it pretty fast, concentrating on that drop.'

KVD: Texas rig 5-inch Ocho in blue craw or green pumpkin — pitch into holes in the grass at 9-11ft. 3/8oz tungsten with 3/0 Mustad grip pin hook. Color doesn't matter much — 'anything green pumpkin.' The Ocho just glides straight down into the clean holes.

KVD: 'For spawning bass in bushes, rig a creature bait with 3/8oz weight and flip fast. Make more flips to cover water. You may not catch every fish but you'll catch several. If the area holds big fish, go back through slower.'
KVD: 'After power-fishing through an area, clean up with a Texas-rigged Game Hawg and light 1/4oz weight. Lighter weight lets the bait fall slower so the appendages dance through the area.'

KVD: 'Late fall, use cranks with tighter wobble and slow down. Series 3 runs 7-8ft with tight action — appeals to big bass in that depth range. Switch to deeper Series 5 when fish are at 10ft. Below 50 degrees they won't react to a burned bait.'

KVD: Gravel Dog for the 6-12ft mid-range zone in cold water. Round body, super wide wobble, loud rattle. Eats up the bottom — perfect for chunk rock banks, bluffs, riprap around bridges. One of the most weedless crankbaits you can throw.

KVD: 'Fall crankbait strategy: target bank transitions where different rock types meet (gravel meets stair-step rocks) — creates natural ambush zones. 3XD hits 12ft, 5XD reaches 15-18ft. Key is consistent bottom contact. Parallel bank transitions to keep bait in the strike zone longer. Works regardless of water clarity.'

KVD: Position boat outside the grass line, fire at a 45° quartering angle into shallower grass. Crank down into the grass, slow up — let the bait float free or rip it free. That stop-start-rip action through grass is 'almost as good as a finesse bait at triggering inactive fish.'

KVD: 'Post-spawn bass slide to secondary points, road beds, shallow ledges, main lake points — the 8-10ft depth range. A Series 5 in Sexy Shad does its best work here. Don't jump to deep divers too early post-spawn — meet bass in the middle with a medium diver, it's less intrusive than aggressive deep plugs.'

KVD: 'The 3XD's small body gets bites that bigger-bodied crankbaits won't. Dives 10-12ft — a key depth zone in late winter/early spring. When things get tough, switch to a Baby Z-Too on a shaky head, swimbait jig head, or drop shot for finesse versatility.'

KVD: 'Mid-range 6-12ft zone: round body crankbait with wide wobble and loud rattle. Must contact bottom — deflection off rock and cover is the biggest strike trigger in cold water. Fish chunk rock banks, riprap, bridge areas, bluffs.'

defaultPresentation.retrieveNote: Crank down into the grass, slow up — let the bait float free or rip it free

tipRules.tip: KVD: 3XD for the deep zone in cold water. Smaller profile, tight action, gets 12-15ft on 10lb fluoro. Fish steeper rocky banks, deeper transition zones. Make long casts to keep the bait in the zone for as long as possible.

KVD: Won Toledo Bend fishing 'stop signs' — clean areas outside major spawning coves with crankbaits. Rotated 10XD, 8XD, and 6XD across three depth ranges. Start shallow early on the shad spawn, then transition to deep cranking for the biggest fish.

KVD: 'Cranking grass edges: position boat just outside grass line, make parallel/quartering casts along it. Use 14-20lb fluorocarbon for strength to rip free. When bait loads up slightly, pause — that tug might be a fish. If not, drop rod tip for slack, sharp snap to free it, continue retrieving. 3XD/Series 5/5XD covers 8-15ft grass edges.'

KVD: 'Great Lakes smallmouth in heavy wind: crank a 5XD in Green Gizzard Shad on 17lb fluoro. The heavier line reduces running depth — keep bait above the fish, not digging bottom. Speed is the trigger, not bottom deflections. Reel fast, add erratic starts/stops/jerks. Drift with the wind using trolling motor drift mode, make long downwind casts.'

KVD: 'First bait on deep bream beds is a crankbait that contacts bottom where beds are — 5XD in Natural Bream color, grinding through the beds fires up big bass lurking around them. Set up where you can see beds on Mega 360.'

KVD: Pick a crankbait that runs just a little deeper than the water you're fishing — that's when it's at its best action. A 5XD in 15ft of water contacts bottom at 13-14ft with great lively action and deflection. The same 5XD in 5ft overpowers the bottom and loses triggering quality.

KVD: 'Deep cranking outside weed grass is my absolute favorite way to fish natural lakes in summer. Deep weed edge can be 8ft in shallow milfoil lakes or 20ft in clear deep lakes. Key: when you snag a little, SNAP the crankbait out of the weeds — that often triggers the fish into biting.'

KVD: 'Smallmouth on deeper flats (15-25ft): use 5XD or 6XD, reel as fast as you can with occasional pauses and rod snaps. It's not a specific target deal — cast in all directions to intercept roaming fish. Use 6.4:1 gear ratio, slightly faster than normal cranking.'
KVD: 'Deep zone 12-15ft: go smaller profile with tight action on 10lb fluoro. This 3XD has produced all over the country, especially on highland reservoirs where bass live deeper in winter.'

KVD: 'As summer day heats up and topwater fades, tie on a 6XD to dig main lake points and deep vegetation to 20ft on 12lb line. Clear water = Blue Gizzard Shad color. Dingy = Sexy Shad. Long 7-10 rod to launch as far as possible — longer cast maximizes diving depth. Slow 5.3:1 reel for deep cranking torque.'
KVD: 'If there's some stain to the water, a deep-running crawfish-pattern crankbait rooted along the bottom is strong in prespawn. Bass feed heavily on crawfish emerging from hibernation.'

presentationRules.retrieveNote: Key: when you snag a little, SNAP the crankbait out of the weeds — that often triggers the fish into biting.'

defaultPresentation.retrieveNote: Use 14-20lb fluorocarbon for strength to rip free

tipRules.tip: KVD: 'Fall smallmouth in the 50s: deep-diving jerkbait, DON'T slow your cadence just because water is cooling. Maintain precise boat control with Spot-Lock, make long casts along key structures, aggressively work the bait. 12lb fluorocarbon on 6-10 medium heavy rod.'

KVD: 'Clear water jerkbait: if water is clear, always have one ready. Fish come up hard from 17-18ft to strike. Use Deep 300 Series for deep clear water. Shallower over grass, switch to shallow-running version. Cast, pop forward, let rod tip fall back toward bait to maintain slack — that gives the perfect erratic side-to-side action. Quick sequences of 2-3 jerks/twitches.'
KVD: 'I caught half a dozen on a jerkbait in 41°F water. When you find one, they're not going to be alone — there's going to be more.'

KVD: 'The right jerkbait cadence varies day to day — some days I wait nearly a minute between twitches, some days only a few seconds. Water temp and weather play a big factor but sometimes it's trial and error. It's all about feel and experience.'

KVD: 'In cold water, I use a solid pull a couple times and pause briefly — point the rod at the bait for slack and enhanced action. Pull the bait short distances vs. jerking hard to run 3-4 feet deeper. One second pause between pulls is plenty for me.'

KVD: 'I prefer real bright colors — sexy shad and chartreuse sexy shad — even in clear water. It's about getting the fish to see the bait when they're far away. I like 3-4 feet of clarity minimum, crystal clear is ideal.'

KVD: 'On natural lakes in early fall, use a crankbait first to cover water and identify key spots. Then switch to a deep jerkbait to triple catch rates — it stays in the strike zone longer and matches the conditions better. Target inside turns cutting into breaklines, not just points.'

KVD: KVD 300 Deep runs 10-12ft — go-to for highland reservoirs and clear natural lakes in cold water. Fish off main lake points, bluff walls, bridge pilings, riprap. Parallel deeper edges. Twitch-twitch-pause with LONG pauses. Bright colors in shad kill conditions so fish see it from distance.

KVD: 'Overcast + wind puts bass into feeding overdrive on clear reservoirs. Target main lake windblown points and where river channel pushes close to shore. Get deep jerkbait to maximum diving depth to call bass from as far as possible. Bright but natural-hued baits (bone, white) on overcast days. Start and finish jerking with slack line.'

KVD: Even in slick calm clear conditions, a jerkbait over the top of them will draw fish from distance. 'They're going to see it and they're going to come.' Don't switch to finesse just because it's calm.

KVD: 'Fall is an especially great jerkbait time — forage has been growing all year, so use bigger jerkbaits. Always fish windy areas over calm spots — wind makes bass aggressive and looking up. Start and finish retrieves with slack line for best side-to-side action. Chrome Sexy Shad on sunny days for maximum flash.'

KVD: 'No other lure can pull bass from farther away. If you can see bottom at 20ft, that's still shallow enough for jerkbaits. Adjust cadence daily — let bass tell you what they want. Cold water = longer pauses, warm water = more active retrieve. Always start erratic.'

KVD: 'Jerkbait is a staple for northern smallmouth power fishing. Even if they don't bite, smallmouth follow baits to the boat and reveal their location. Go back and finesse them later. Power fishing catches smallies AND helps find them.'

KVD: 'Not every bass swims to the back of creeks in fall. Keep your boat in 10 feet, cast up to 4-5 feet on shallow flat main lake secondary points. Quality reel for long smooth casts, 12lb fluorocarbon.'

KVD: Jerkbait is a major player when Tennessee River is clear. Fish parallel down riprap, causeways, grass edges, bridge pilings. Efficient at covering different sections. The start-and-stop retrieve is 'absolutely deadly' this time of year — keeps bait in the strike zone for a long time.

KVD: 'Late winter/cold spring with snowmelt runoff makes water clearer than normal — great prespawn jerkbait period. Target flats near deep water access, not shallow creek backs. Focus on northerly flats that get the longest sun benefit. Dark/muck bottoms, rocky areas, dying vegetation make them warmer.'

KVD: 'Deep jerkbait is a search lure for big-water smallmouth on huge flats. Use MEGA 360 to find bottom composition changes, then direct high-percentage casts. Forward-facing sonar gives real-time feedback — adjust cadence, color, or running depth until you trigger fish. Switch to dropshot when bass follow but won't commit.'
KVD: 'In clear cold water, the jerkbait may be the better choice over lipless or spinnerbait. You can alter its action by jerking, ripping, or pausing to interest neutral fish.'

KVD: 'Spring jerkbait: work it on slack line — never go the same direction twice. Pop forward, pause, point rod tip back at bait to maintain slack, repeat. The pause is deadly — bait just suspends like a dying shad and fish can't resist. Biggest mistake is just steadily jerking and reeling back.'

KVD: 'Jerkbaits really shine in clear water reservoirs because they attract bass visually. Very few lures outperform a jerkbait in late winter/early spring. Standard 300 series around docks and under 10ft; 300D for 15-25ft depths. On herring lakes, bass are nomadic — need to stay on the move with power baits.'

KVD: 'When bass are high in the water column with some clarity, the deep diving jerkbait is my favorite for suspended fish. They still relate to structure features like points, ledges, and humps — just not on the bottom.'

KVD: 'In cold clear water, the jerkbait is probably the very best lure you can tie on. Jerks then long pauses give cold lethargic fish time to react. Crystal Shad for super clear, Sexy Shad for barely stained. Deep 300 dives to 10ft on 10lb line. Not for muddy water.'
KVD: 'Suspending jerkbaits are my favorite winter artificials. You can fish them in the same place for long periods — extremely deadly on suspending bass.'
KVD: 'As the water cools to the lower end of the autumn range, a suspending jerkbait will catch fish.'

KVD: 'Spring jerkbait: work it on slack line — never go the same direction twice

tipRules.tip: KVD: 'Drop shot lets me cover water faster than typical finesse — cast, hop a few times, swim it, let it fall back. For clear water, use smaller subtle baits with lots of action. Use No. 2 hook through the nose with flat side down for darting/swimming action. Around brush, Texas-rig on 1/0 Grip Pin Edge hook.'

KVD: 'Drop shot works deep AND shallow — I fish it on 6-8ft flats just as effectively. Use a long cylindrical weight that pulls through vegetation and rocks. Tungsten for clean bottoms. Nose-hook a straight-tailed worm for unbelievable action. Use heavy enough weight to maintain consistent bottom contact — if 1/4oz isn't cutting it, go to 3/8oz.'

KVD: 'Drop-shot excels over muddy, mucky, slimy bottoms — suspends the bait above gunk. Favor it for vertical fishing under the boat, video gaming on graph. Heavier weight for fast drop without impacting bait action — good for heavy current too.'

KVD: 'Drop shot is my primary finesse rig day in and day out. Known as clear-water technique but also efficient at triggering non-aggressive bass in stained water once you've located a school. Far more versatile than most give it credit for — can use large Texas-rigged baits in cover for big largemouths. 8-10lb line, tungsten weight just heavy enough to keep tension.'

KVD: 'Never go smallmouth fishing without a dropshot tied on. When fish chase power baits to the boat but won't commit, drop down to them with a dropshot. Use 3/8 to 1/2oz weight for cleanup finesse work on finicky smallmouth.'

KVD: Drop shot with dice/bug baits (Sexy Bug, Tumbleweed) is a game-changer. Use the lightest weight possible — 1/8oz ideal. The slow fall with all that action is what makes it deadly. Great during Mayfly hatches. Fish annihilate it on the fall.

KVD: 'Drop shot is one of my favorites for covering deep and shallow flats when I'm uncertain where bass are holding. Use a heavier sinker — cast farther, get to bottom quickly, work it a few times, make more casts. A faster fall can trigger strikes better than slow fall when fish are neutral or negative.'

KVD: 'The drop-shot is one of the unique ways — especially for clear water lakes — to cover water from shallow to deep, and fast. It gets the bait down in a hurry yet you can still make a finesse presentation.'
KVD: 'With a half-ounce weight, the bait gets down to 20-30 feet in just a few seconds. Shake it a few times, see if you can get a bite, then move on. I can cover a ton of water this way.'
KVD: 'When the bite is super tough, wacky rig a 4-inch Ocho or Dream Shot on the drop shot. For power-shotting, go with something that has a lot of wiggle — Baby Z-Too or nose-hooked Dream Shot.'

defaultTips.tip: KVD: 'I throw a drop-shot in 2 feet of water as much as 40. It's a great presentation for all scenarios — a really good way to cover water AND catch fish when the bite is tough.'

Shake it a few times, see if you can get a bite, then move on

tipRules.tip: KVD: 'On bright days in clear water, run your spinnerbait fast — rip it for reaction strikes so bass can't inspect it. Slow down on overcast days or in turbid water.'
KVD: 'Run your spinnerbait into cover — docks, vegetation, logs. The erratic motion triggers more strikes. In open water, give sporadic jerks for the same effect. Bump it.'

KVD: 'Big 3/4oz spinnerbait with single willow blade for offshore structure — let it helicopter down on a tight line feeling the blade turn, rip it off the bottom, let it flutter back. Mimics fleeing shad. 7-foot rod for big jumps.'

KVD: 'Slow-roll spinnerbaits along tapering grass lines, standing timber, submerged structures. Reel just fast enough to skim cover. When it touches an obstacle, speed up and let it flutter back — that provokes the reaction strike.'
KVD: Spinnerbait is one of the key baits for shallow grass flats alongside the Thunder Cricket and Hybrid Hunter.

KVD: 'High muddy water = spinnerbait time. Use bulky spinnerbait with Colorado blades — more vibration helps fish find it, provides lift, requires slower wind for more time in strike zone. Bright colors: fire tiger, chartreuse, fluorescent orange/yellow, pearl white. Black works too for silhouette in dirty water.'

KVD: 'Fall — burn a spinnerbait just beneath the surface in clear water. Use compact baits with smaller blades (3/16oz, small Colorado/willow or single Colorado) to match the smaller young-of-year baitfish. Keep it in the upper water column.'
KVD: 'In stained fall water, go bigger with Colorado blades for more vibration. For grass like hydrilla/milfoil, use larger baits with willowleaf blades.'

KVD: 'During shad spawn (~72 degrees, after bass spawn), a willowleaf spinnerbait is one of the best lures. You'll feel shad ticking against it and see them following it back. Also use Thunder Cricket, white swim jig, or buzzbait depending on cover. Once sun comes out, switch to jerkbait or crankbait as fish ease deeper.'
KVD: 'For northern smallmouth, a 1/2oz Burner spinnerbait is one of my top three power lures. It locates fish fast — smallmouth that won't commit still show themselves following it to the boat.'
KVD: 'In fall I often downsize to a 3/8 or 5/16oz Finesse spinnerbait with smaller blades to match baitfish size. Target channel-swing banks, mud flats, and backs of creeks where shad hang out.'

KVD: 'Fall spinnerbait: super versatile, top to bottom. Burn it right under surface in clear water, slow-roll around laydowns and logs, fish around docks and bushes. Usually double willow blade in fall. Heavy rain or dirty water in creek backs, switch to Colorado blade. Always match color to the forage.'
KVD: 'Spinnerbait and vibrating jig are both productive in stained winter water — they move lots of water. Target laydowns, docks, and rock banks with visible cover.'

KVD: 'During spawn, burn a spinnerbait around shallow bushes in pockets. Spawners may not bite but they'll swat at it, giving away their location. Then switch to a creature bait and dunk into those bushes to make them bite.'

KVD: 'Pre-spawn grass and brush cover (cattails, lily pads, laydowns, bushes) — spinnerbait is the go-to. Slow-roll through thick cover. Dirty water = bright colors (chartreuse) with Colorado blades for max vibration. Clear water = natural colors (bluegill, pearl white, shad). Slow retrieve keeps bait in strike zone longer.'

KVD: 'Rain is my favorite time to power fish — #1 bait is a spinnerbait or Thunder Cricket. Rain creates low light that gives bass an advantage over prey. Strike zone gets bigger. Don't just reel baits straight in — hit stuff, rip out of grass, bounce off dock pilings, stop and start. Quick erratic movements trigger fish.'

KVD: 'Don't overlook spinnerbaits for suspended bass. A 1oz Bottom Dweller counted down lets me fish through the 15-25ft zone, winding it just above suspended fish. Maintain a specific depth throughout the retrieve.'
KVD: 'A slow-rolled spinnerbait works well in prespawn. I pick up my pace at around 52°F, running down a bank quickly throwing a spinnerbait.'
KVD: 'Postspawn in stained water, a tandem Colorado or Colorado/Indiana spinnerbait is excellent.'
KVD: 'Fall lure preferences include spinnerbaits with willow leaf blades — they match the profile of baitfish.'

KVD: Colorado/Indiana combo for stained or dirty water — maximum vibration with still a lot of flash. Half-ounce size to get deeper in the water column. Slow roll over cover, keep bait above bass's line of sight.
KVD: Colorado/Willow for cleaner water. Half-ounce still works. Slow pulsing vibration is the key — keep that bait in the strike zone as long as possible.

KVD: Slow rolling a spinnerbait around steep rocky banks and laydowns in cold water. First Bassmaster win was in December at Lake Lanier doing exactly this. Use a plastic trailer — 'absolutely paramount' in cold winter months.

defaultTips.tip: KVD: Slow rolling is one of the most underestimated powerful tools. Keep bait above the bass's line of sight over cover. Barely turn the handle. Speed up through limbs — the flutter on the other side is when they bite.

KVD: 'Big 3/4oz spinnerbait with single willow blade for offshore structure — let it helicopter down on a tight line ...

tipRules.tip: KVD: Half-ounce Thunder Cricket in white with blade minnow trailer — cut the head off to shorten it. Water rushes around the blade and hits the tail at the perfect distance for lively action.
KVD: 'When that water gets into the mid to upper 40s in March, you're going to start catching them with a Thunder Cricket. That's when fish move out of wintering areas and start patrolling flats.'

KVD: Thunder Cricket in shallow grass even in upper 30s water. Use 3/8 oz (lighter than normal) with straight-tail blade minnow trailer for subtle action. Pull and stop, not steady retrieve. Get it down in old dead vegetation — it holds heat and bass are drawn to it. Tungsten model has smaller blade, tighter vibration, runs deeper — good for tough conditions.

KVD: 'Pre-spawn Thunder Cricket in grass: wind it really slowly — you should barely feel it vibrate. If it thumps your rod hard, you're going too fast. Use it as a wakebait that just barely gets into the grass, jerk it out of the grass for more bites. Lighter weight for shallow grass, heavier for deeper. Blade Minnow trailer in bluegill color for grassy conditions.'

KVD: 'Thunder Cricket speed adjustment: if the bait just feels heavier or loses vibration, fish are eating but not aggressively — be dialed in to notice. Have multiple weights on the deck (3/8, 1/2, 5/8oz). Heavier = fish faster while staying down. Lighter = slower, longer in strike zone. Tungsten version has tighter vibration for tougher, less aggressive conditions.'

KVD: Thunder Cricket is a great search bait for shallow grass flats in practice. Cast around scattered patches and holes — if there's a group of active fish, one should come up and bite it. Also good as a backup plan when sight fishing conditions deteriorate (wind, clouds).

KVD: 'Thunder Cricket is a reaction bait — its blade vibration and flash trigger instinctual strikes even when bass are lethargic in cold water. Use a lift-and-drop retrieve with brief pauses. Most hits come on the drop.'
KVD: 'When wind pushes into shallow flats, bass edge up to feed. Fish the edges of grass, rocks, or flats where bait accumulates. Fish shallow first, then work deeper on windy days.'

KVD: 'Before I ever got there, I had a Thunder Cricket on my mind.' Pre-spawn at 55° water with shallow grass — perfect for a bladed jig. Fish the outside edge of the grass, get it down, nice and slow. When you find one they're grouped — pre-spawn fish have buddies.

KVD: Clear water = natural colors on the Thunder Cricket. Green pumpkin or blue craw body with KVD Magic trailer (bite the head off to shorten). Imitates bluegill and crayfish — looks natural to the bass. Cover water and get it in the zone.

KVD: 'Thunder Cricket vibrating jig with Blade Minnow trailer is a tremendous big bass catcher in fall. I use it in clear water — it's a great smallmouth bait. Gives off a lot of vibration, very different than a squarebill.'

KVD: 'In shallower prespawn areas around cover, a Rage Blade (bladed jig), swim jig, Red Eye Shad, or squarebill are my choices. These cover the shallower zone while jerkbaits handle the staging areas on the flat.'
KVD: 'Around bluegill beds, use a 3/8oz Thunder Cricket tipped with a Blade Minnow. 95% of the time green pumpkin. Start on the outer edge of spawning beds — that's where big bass hang out.'

KVD: 'Thunder Cricket shines in pre-spawn when water temps are mid-to-upper 50s. Fish it around grass or any aquatic vegetation — goes through grass better than a squarebill. Can be skipped underneath docks. Comes in four sizes to imitate shad or bream. Catches fish year-round.'

KVD: Post-frontal cold pre-spawn — work the Thunder Cricket like a jig. Get it down in the grass, hop it, twitch it. Let it hit bottom or grass, then twitch. 'Sometimes you got to make them react.' Bites are subtle — just gets heavy or the vibration goes away.
KVD: When wind is blowing and it's cloudy, fish are aggressive with a bigger strike zone — just wind the Thunder Cricket along. But calm/post-frontal = slow way down, work it like a jig.

defaultTips.tip: KVD: 'Keep contact with the bottom — reaction bites happen low in the water column. Change cadence often. Adjust rod angle to feel blade vibration without snagging.'

tipRules.tip: KVD: 'In dirty water, pick a swim jig color that stands out — either really bright or really dark. Black and blue is always a good go-to. Rage Craw trailer with thick flanges displaces a lot of water, helps fish find it.'

KVD: 'Swim jig is the first bait I reach for on unfamiliar water with shallow cover. Swim it fast like a spinnerbait OR pitch it like a regular jig around docks. It's so efficient for covering water — a mainstay in every pro's arsenal.'

KVD: 'A Rage Swimmer scuffed along the bottom is deadly in cold water. Just scoot it along — looks like a perch, bluegill, or crawfish. Fish it DOWN on the bottom, slow hopping. Night and day difference in bites vs. swimming it through the water column.'

KVD: 'Fish swim jig up high in the water column, shaking it along. Rage Craw trailer has wide swimming action that helps keep it high. Three basic colors you need: black and blue, bluegill, and shad. Fish on braided line around grass, scattered pads, bushes, boat docks. Works everywhere there's shallow cover.'

KVD: 'Skip cast swim jigs with baitcasting gear into tight spaces — docks, catwalks, bushes, overhanging trees, under pontoons. Heavier tackle lets you pull bass away from heavy cover quickly. First cast is critical: stealth provokes reaction bite from fish in shadows. Use heavy line (50lb braid or 20lb fluoro).'

KVD: 'For skipping swim jigs around shallow cover and vegetation, use 1/4 or 3/8oz Heavy Cover Swim Jig. In colored water, 50lb braid. Sparser cover or clearer water, 20lb fluorocarbon. Deep docks where jig falls to bottom in clear water, bump down to 14lb fluoro. Rage Bug trailer for lift and pulsing action.'

KVD: 'Swim jig versatility: while casting and reeling, spot a target and pitch it into a bush, hole in the grass, or heavy cover — fish it like a flipping jig. Transition from horizontal to vertical presentation with the same bait. Always opt for heavier jig rather than lighter for the reaction strike advantage.'

presentationRules.retrieveNote: KVD: 'In dirty water, pick a swim jig color that stands out — either really bright or really dark

defaultPresentation.retrieveNote: KVD: 'Swim jig is the first bait I reach for on unfamiliar water with shallow cover

tipRules.tip: KVD: 'I threw the Ned rig out and immediately caught one on that in 43°F water.'
KVD: 'Ned rig is a huge player on clear water lakes with clean rocky bottoms and mixed species. Absolute deal in goby-infested Great Lakes waters. Great cleanup bait for smallmouth.'
KVD: 'Ned rig with 1/8oz head is a smallmouth finesse staple. Great cleanup bait when conditions call for subtle presentation or fish won't react to aggressive offerings.'
KVD: In clear water, they'll bite the Ned Rig on the fall. Cast out, let it sink on dead slack line — don't tighten up until it hits bottom.
KVD: In heavy wind and deep water, bump up to 3/16 oz Ned head. In shallower water, use 1/16 oz — lighter the head, the better the action.

defaultTips.tip: KVD: Cast, let it sink on dead slack line, then just drag real slow keeping the jig head on the bottom. The mushroom head makes it stand up — that's what's special. If you miss one, immediately drop it right back — they'll come back and bite it.

tipRules.tip: KVD: 'When debris prevents spinnerbait fishing in muddy water, switch to a 1/2oz flipping jig in black/blue with blue craw Rage Craw trailer. Flip into thick cover or swim it depending on the area. White jig is another option.'

KVD: 'Punching matted vegetation: use 1.5oz tungsten weight pegged with bobber stop, 65lb braid, 4/0 hook with a Punch Bug. Cast high to build momentum through the mat. Rod at 10 o'clock, slack line for free fall. Give 3-4 shakes under the mat, sometimes lift bait just under surface to tempt a bite.'

KVD: 'For heaviest cover, vertical presentations: 3/4oz Hack Attack jig with Rage Craw trailer. Fast fall mimics bowling ball — prompts quick reaction, no time to think. Smaller trailer (Scon Bug, Menace) increases fall rate. For thickest mats, use big weight with single hook and punch bug instead — penetrates better than a flipping jig.'

presentationRules.trailer: blue with blue craw Rage Craw
presentationRules.retrieveNote: Flip into thick cover or swim it depending on the area

defaultPresentation.trailer: 4oz Hack Attack jig with Rage Craw

tipRules.tip: KVD: 'For deep bream beds in summer (10-20ft), drag a 3/4oz football jig with Rage Craw trailer in blue craw/red flake. Also try a swinging football head jig with Rage Menace. Drag slowly in and out of individual craters. Fish lurk on outsides of beds waiting for bluegill to stray too far from home base.'

presentationRules.retrieveNote: KVD: 'For deep bream beds in summer (10-20ft), drag a 3/4oz football jig with Rage Craw trailer in blue craw/red flake

tipRules.tip: KVD: 'In clearer water on deep bream beds, go finesse with a shaky head and Fat Baby Finesse Worm in blue craw/red flake. Drag slowly in and out of individual bed craters. Cast far beyond the bed complex and drag through.'

KVD: 'Shaky head is my choice for casting to specific targets — bushes, seawalls, riprap, standing timber, pilings, docks. Best over clean hard bottom (sand, gravel, rock) and generally 15 feet or less for me.'

KVD: 'Power finesse with shaky head: make precise casts to where bass should be, shake it a few times, quickly recast. Narrow the pattern to specific key areas — 80% of bites on a certain structure type, on first couple casts. Fish the meat of a spot quickly and move to the next one.'

presentationRules.retrieveNote: Drag slowly in and out of individual bed craters

defaultPresentation.retrieveNote: KVD: 'Power finesse with shaky head: make precise casts to where bass should be, shake it a few times, quickly recast

tipRules.tip: KVD: 'Don't overlook an old-school Carolina-rigged lizard through deep bream beds. The weightless presentation of the lizard hovering over the beds imitates bedding bream better than anything else sometimes.'

KVD: Blade bait is my #1 bait for covering water in super cold water. Fish get locked on the bottom — short hop it on edges, drops, outside edges of flats and weed lines. Use just heavy enough to get to the zone. Great for largemouth, smallmouth, and spots.
KVD: 'Metal blade baits probe vertical and fast-sloping structure efficiently in winter.'

presentationRules.retrieveNote: Fish get locked on the bottom — short hop it on edges, drops, outside edges of flats and weed lines

tipRules.tip: KVD: 'Sexy Dawg walking bait for covering water quickly in clear water and open areas — big grass flats, rocky points, long casts. That walking action really draws them. Good during shad and herring spawns. Use translucent colors in clear water. Line: mono, not braid, for topwater poppers (14lb small, 17-20lb large).'

KVD: 'Fall is all about topwater. Use a Sexy Dawg to cast to surfacing activity — the Hard Knock version so bass hear it too. 7-4 MH fast tip rod, 40-50lb braid for optimum retrieve. Best shad activity often comes in the afternoon in fall.'

KVD: 'A 4.5" Sexy Dawg has incredible drawing power — you can attract bass from far away in clear to stained water, as long as there's not too much wave action. Use 50lb braid, 7.3:1 reel — braid makes it easier to walk and gets better hook-ups.'

KVD: 'Post-spawn bass suspend around cover like docks and bushes while recovering. Best lures prey on their guarding instincts — walking stickbaits, topwaters, jerkbaits. They have built-in action and stay in the strike zone longer. Avoid bottom baits since fish suspend during this period.'

KVD: 'Always have a Sexy Dawg topwater tied on in summer. No matter how hot, there are always shallow bass willing to hit topwater at sunrise or sunset. Use 40lb braid — helps it walk better on surface AND gives firmer hooksets at end of long casts vs mono. 7-4 rod with soft forgiving tip to keep fish hooked.'
KVD: 'Postspawn in clear water, topwaters work great — they stay in the strike zone a long time, a big plus when bass aren't very aggressive.'
KVD: 'Always keep a topwater lure, especially a noisy chugger, tied on in fall. Bass often school on the surface chasing baitfish.'

presentationRules.retrieveNote: Use 50lb braid, 7.3:1 reel — braid makes it easier to walk and gets better hook-ups.'
Use 40lb braid — helps it walk better on surface AND gives firmer hooksets at end of long casts vs mono

tipRules.tip: KVD: 'Neko rig is excellent on gunky bottoms — hook above weight stays clean in the strike zone. Drop it vertical under the boat or cast out around. Use lead nail weights and cut excess to perfect the fall. 3/32oz tungsten for deep.'

KVD: When it goes slick calm and fish get finicky, switch the same Ocho to a wacky rig on a spinning rod. Cast it out, let it painfully sink to the bottom, twitch a few times — they usually eat it before it hits bottom.

KVD: In clear water, use dice/bug baits (Sexy Bug, Tumbleweed) on a Neko rig — incredible action and visibility. In stained water, stick to the traditional Neko worm for a subtler profile. Superglue the tungsten weights so they don't fly out when fish jump.

defaultTips.tip: KVD: Every serious angler needs a Neko rig. Wacky-hook through the middle, weight in the nose for straight fall. Superglue weights in place. Lead wire wrapped on the hook shank adds subtle weight without bulk. Use 8lb braid to 8lb fluoro leader.

presentationRules.retrieveNote: Cast it out, let it painfully sink to the bottom, twitch a few times — they usually eat it before it hits bottom

tipRules.tip: KVD: 'Dog days suspended bass: if crankbait doesn't fire them up, try a spoon or hair jig that falls fast to where fish are. Rip it and let it fall back — resembles a dying shad. Hit a lot of spots, catch a few, move on. Rotate back to the same spots 3-4 times a day.'

KVD: 'For suspended bass, rip a jigging spoon or flutter spoon above the fish and let it fall back. Also works with swimbaits on jig heads — 3/8oz for higher fish, 3/4oz for deeper. Choose a lure that stays in their strike zone and maintain that depth throughout the cast.'

presentationRules.retrieveNote: Rip it and let it fall back — resembles a dying shad
KVD: 'For suspended bass, rip a jigging spoon or flutter spoon above the fish and let it fall back

tipRules.tip: KVD: 'Skipping buzzbaits under docks has been a secret on tour for years. Use compact frame designs that skip well. Gets into dark haunts that have never heard the gurgle of a buzzbait — an unconventional technique that's definitely different on pressured water.'

KVD: 'Early spring muddy water: 1/2oz Structure Jig to slowly probe every piece of shallow cover. Black/blue for muddy water. Clearer water, drag it slowly across rocky bottom on primary or secondary points — green pumpkin. Super versatile lure for late winter/early spring.'
