tipRules.tip: KVD: 'Bass relate to cover like stumps strongly in prespawn, especially in murky lakes. A jig-and-pig rules here.'

grass.advice: KVD: 'Lakes that have grass change the most.' When hydrilla clumps to the surface, fish offshore grass in the middle of the lake — that's where the bait and big fish are in summer. They don't get up by the shore when it's hot.

KVD: In extreme cold on grass lakes, fish don't go deep — they stay shallow in flats with pad stems in 1-2ft of water even in upper 30s temps. Dead vegetation holds heat. Look for staging areas with slightly deeper water adjacent to spawning flats.

KVD: '45° angle trick' — position outside the grass line, cast quartering into shallower grass. The crankbait follows the contour/taper of the grass line as it comes back to you. You can feel every irregularity — subtle points, cuts, where grass comes out farther or gets taller. Fish relate to those irregularities. Mark the spot when you get bit — they won't be alone.

KVD: 'Summer — baitfish and bass gravitate to weedy bays and pockets where water is cooler and oxygenated. Bass position near irregularities: holes, pockets, and points in the weed bed. Weedless frog on top, or flip plastics through the grass.'

KVD: 'Natural lakes in fall — start on outside weed edge of biggest flat on main lake. Crank finds irregularities in the weedline, then pitch finesse baits to those spots. Inside turns are where you find concentrations — the closer to the bank that turn is, the better. Bass can stay shallow in milfoil/coontail even in the 40-degree range.'

advice.tip: KVD: 'Offshore humps in 10-20ft are prime areas for deep bream beds in summer. Scan with side imaging — the beds look like large waffles underwater. Any cover on these spots (rock, shell, brush, scattered logs) makes them even more attractive.'

bluff.advice: KVD: 'Winter bass relate strongly to vertical and fast-sloping structure — rock bluffs, river channel dropoffs, standing timber, 45-degree banks. These let bass make major depth changes without swimming far. In prespawn, fish the ends of bluffs close to shallower water.'

KVD: In cold water, target transition areas where bass will stage before moving to spawning bays. Channel swing ends, flat edges, steeper banks transitioning to flatter areas, where different rock types come together. Chunk rock banks, bluffs, and riprap around bridges are prime for the mid-range crankbait.

KVD: Guntersville breaks into three sections: Lower end = rocks, bluffs, bridges (good for jerkbaits, cranking). Mid-lake = river ledges, miles of grass (always a player). Upper end = shallow creeks, lily pads (Bill Lowen territory — guys who get way back into places others don't look).

KVD: Gravel Dog for the 6-12ft mid-range zone in cold water. Round body, super wide wobble, loud rattle. Eats up the bottom — perfect for chunk rock banks, bluffs, riprap around bridges. One of the most weedless crankbaits you can throw.

KVD: 'Mid-range 6-12ft zone: round body crankbait with wide wobble and loud rattle. Must contact bottom — deflection off rock and cover is the biggest strike trigger in cold water. Fish chunk rock banks, riprap, bridge areas, bluffs.'

point.advice: KVD: 'Summer — bass migrate to the main lake and hang around channel-oriented structure: humps, points, dropoffs, ledges. Your best bet early summer is a slow-tapering point at the mouth of a spawning cove. In fall, bass move to long points with a gradual taper.'

KVD: 'Fall reservoirs — shad move shallow to warmest water in creek arms. Go as far up a creek as your boat allows. If it has a defined channel, bass use it for ambush points. Pods of bait near surface plus birds indicate a really good afternoon bite as sun warms water. Fall drawdown exposes cover you can't see in summer.'

KVD: In clear deep lakes with shad kills, fish often suspend. The higher they are in the water column, the more active they are. Fish off main lake points, humps, drops, centers of big bays — not near the bank. Alabama rig with spinners (keeps rig up in water column) or minnow/swimbait on spotlight head are best for covering water around suspended fish.

KVD: In northern natural lakes, perch and gobies are the primary forage — fish spend most of their time feeding on them. Both live on or near rocks. Birds diving on shallow points = alewives spawning (another forage clue).

KVD: During pre-spawn, when you find one fish on a spot, they're usually grouped. Got a bite every single cast on one little spot on a point. They stage together before moving up to spawn. Mark the spot and work it thoroughly.

flat.advice: KVD: 'Bass spawn on flat, protected structure — large expanses of shallow water (10ft or less on lowland reservoirs, 8-20ft on highland reservoirs). Look for areas where contour lines are spread far apart, not close together.'

KVD: Bass in cold water really use their lateral line to hunt. Even in dirty water they can feel the pressure waves of a flatside bait. This is why flatsided crankbaits are 'magical' in cold water — the flat sides displace a lot of water.

KVD: Red Eye Shad sizes for depth control — 1/4oz for ultra-shallow (1-2ft), 3/8oz for shallow flats, 1/2oz as go-to, 3/4oz for 10-15ft deep drains. Plus tungsten Two Tap in 1/2 and 3/4oz — heavier tungsten fishes slightly deeper. Most anglers get stuck on 1/2oz and don't downsize when fish are shallow.

KVD: Focus on large flats and spawning flats — where fish are coming and going. Look for deeper depressions, edges, drops, centers of drains within the flat. These subtle depth changes concentrate pre-spawn fish. At places like Toledo Bend and Sam Rayburn, fish deeper drains/ditches leading into spawning bays with 3/4oz.

KVD: Flatside crankbaits send off a lot of pressure waves because flat sides displace a ton of water. Best in cold water (bass rely on lateral line to hunt) and dirty water any season (visibility is down). Round body crankbaits vary widely depending on bill angle — the body shape alone doesn't tell the whole story.

creek-channel.advice: KVD: 'Fall — follow the shad migration into creek arms. Fish the first third early fall, then press farther back as temps drop. Idle up watching your graph for suspended shad. Isolated wood and docks in the backs of creek arms are dependable fall patterns.'

KVD: 'Choose a crankbait that runs slightly deeper than the water — that gives best action and triggering when banging off bottom. If water is 10ft, I want bait running 11ft. Manage with bait selection, line size, and rod angle. Multiple rods rigged with different line sizes — smaller for deeper, bigger for shallower. Hold rod up for shallower over ledge tops, lower for depth increases.'

KVD: 'The dream scenario for a squarebill in fall is a rocky creek channel swing in the back of a major tributary, with shad active near the surface. It's an efficient bait because bass are moving shallow and will bite it in just about any color of water.'

KVD: 'Pre-spawn rocky areas and channel swings: squarebill is unbeatable. Use crawfish patterns — Delta Red, Fire Craw, Brown Crawfish, Red Tomato. Position boat close to bank and make short, accurate parallel casts to keep bait in the strike zone.'

dock.advice: KVD: GS1 spinning rod, 6'10" medium action for accuracy around docks. Longer spinning rod for open water distance. 8lb braid to 8lb fluoro leader. Mustad Apex weedless hook for cover/docks, exposed hook in open water. Mustad Apex drop shot hook (wide gap) for creature baits.

KVD: 'For skip casting, use the heaviest line you can get away with — heavier line makes line management easier. 50lb braid for frogs and swim jigs in colored water. 20lb fluorocarbon for sparser cover or clearer water. 14lb fluoro for finesse jigs on deep docks in clear water. Less line on the spool is better for skip casting.'

KVD: 'Postspawn bass hang around their spawning grounds near cover sticking up vertically — dock pilings, submerged bushes, standing tree trunks. Keep your presentation up high in the water column, not bumping bottom.'

KVD: 'When bass are dirt shallow near spawn, use the 1.5 Shallow — runs about 12 inches below surface. Throw on heavy 17lb line near shallow cover and docks. Rod tip high and it practically doubles as a wakebait. Use bluegill/sunfish colors — bass are targeting them around beds.'

KVD: 'In clearer fall creek arms, switch from Sexy Shad to Summer Sexy Shad color on a 1.5 squarebill. Fish it around laydowns, windy banks, and especially shallow floating boat docks in October/November. It's the shad migration toward back of major creeks — but if water is clearer than usual, the subtler color is better.'

laydown.advice: KVD: 'What I look for in a squarebill is erratic swimming — kick around, don't run straight. Old wood baits had this but broke fast. The body and weighting design make every single one swim erratically out of the package. It looks like an injured baitfish — catches a lot of smallmouth over deep water.'

KVD: 'For vertical deep water (50ft on Table Rock, fishing standing timber at 30ft down), use a short rod. Video gaming — looking at graph, dropping to the fish, watching bait on sonar, trying to talk them into biting.'

KVD: 'Power fishing the spawn: use a squarebill to locate stumps, then make persistent casts from various angles. Slow the retrieve to keep bait banging on cover for erratic deflection. Several big fish came after 4+ casts to the same stump. If snagged, snap free from a distance or break off — never go over the stump and disturb the area.'
KVD: 'Bass relate to cover like stumps strongly in prespawn, especially in murky lakes. A jig-and-pig rules here.'

knowledge.insight: KVD: 'Fall reservoirs — shad move shallow to warmest water in creek arms. Go as far up a creek as your boat allows. If it has a defined channel, bass use it for ambush points. Pods of bait near surface plus birds indicate a really good afternoon bite as sun warms water. Fall drawdown exposes cover you can't see in summer.'

KVD: 'Natural lakes in fall — start on outside weed edge of biggest flat on main lake. Crank finds irregularities in the weedline, then pitch finesse baits to those spots. Inside turns are where you find concentrations — the closer to the bank that turn is, the better. Bass can stay shallow in milfoil/coontail even in the 40-degree range.'

KVD: 'Fall retrieve progression: go fast and erratic early in fall, less erratic late. The later in fall, the more important it is to hit bottom and keep it there at a slow, steady pace — just enough speed to keep the bill thumping. Use cranks right until ice-up but switch to tighter wobble action below 50 degrees.'

KVD: 'Always look for baitfish in fall — herons, seagulls, terns, bait on the graph, visible bait in the water. Seeing shad on top indicates a really good afternoon bite. From there, look for the type of cover the lake has in the areas the most abundant baitfish want to use.'

KVD: For Knoxville Classic (Fort Loudoun/Tellico, mid-March), the key baits are: Red Eye Shad, flatside crankbait (Hardliner 35), jerkbait, and Thunder Cricket. Power fishing will dominate. Most winning fish will come from Loudoun with largemouth. Fish are staging to spawn but cold winter has them behind schedule. Forward-facing sonar will add 1-2 extra fish per day but won't change the core pattern.

KVD: At Knoxville, running to Tellico costs ~3 hours of fishing time per day. In a 3-day event needing 17-18lbs/day to win, you can't afford that. The winner will likely stay on Loudoun and maximize casts. Time management wins classics.

KVD: Pre-spawn one-two punch strategy: shallow crankbait or jerkbait on deeper edges outside pockets, then Thunder Cricket and Hardliner back in the bays where fish are moving to spawn. The combo covers both staging and migrating fish.

KVD: Three depth zones need three crankbaits in cold water. Shallow = flatside (Hardliner). Mid-range 6-12ft = round body wide wobble (Gravel Dog). Deep 12-15ft = smaller tight-action diver (3XD). Each zone demands a different action and profile.

KVD: In cold water cranking, use slow to medium retrieve. Start and stop often — any time during the retrieve, pausing can trigger a following bass into biting. Parallel the bank for long casts to keep the bait in the zone the entire cast.
KVD: Use lighter line in cold water cranking. Lighter line allows the bait to have its liveliest action and reach maximum depth. 10lb fluoro for the 3XD deep zone.

KVD: CC4 medium-heavy with a soft tip for cranking. 6.2:1 gear ratio reel (Paramag RX braking). The soft tip throws small cold-water crankbaits well and allows long casts for maximum time in the zone.

KVD: Bass in cold water really use their lateral line to hunt. Even in dirty water they can feel the pressure waves of a flatside bait. This is why flatsided crankbaits are 'magical' in cold water — the flat sides displace a lot of water.

KVD: Crawfish patterns dominate cold water. Clear water = natural/ghost crawfish (greens and browns). Stained = fire craw, brighter patterns like spring craw. If you see shad activity, shad patterns work too. Look at what color crawfish the fish are eating — is it green, brown, red, or purple? Match it.

KVD: In cold water, target transition areas where bass will stage before moving to spawning bays. Channel swing ends, flat edges, steeper banks transitioning to flatter areas, where different rock types come together. Chunk rock banks, bluffs, and riprap around bridges are prime for the mid-range crankbait.

KVD: 'Making sure your crankbait is contacting the bottom in any depth zone you're fishing — that is absolutely paramount.' The deflection off rocks, cover, even gravel makes a big difference in triggering cold water fish to bite.

KVD: For Knoxville Classic (Fort Loudoun/Tellico, mid-March), the key baits are: Red Eye Shad, flatside crankbait (Hardliner 35), jerkbait, and Thunder Cricket. Power fishing will dominate. Most winning fish will come from Loudoun with largemouth. Fish are staging to spawn but cold winter has them behind schedule. Forward-facing sonar will add 1-2 extra fish per day but won't change the core pattern.

KVD: At Knoxville, running to Tellico costs ~3 hours of fishing time per day. In a 3-day event needing 17-18lbs/day to win, you can't afford that. The winner will likely stay on Loudoun and maximize casts. Time management wins classics.

KVD: Pre-spawn one-two punch strategy: shallow crankbait or jerkbait on deeper edges outside pockets, then Thunder Cricket and Hardliner back in the bays where fish are moving to spawn. The combo covers both staging and migrating fish.

KVD: Three depth zones need three crankbaits in cold water. Shallow = flatside (Hardliner). Mid-range 6-12ft = round body wide wobble (Gravel Dog). Deep 12-15ft = smaller tight-action diver (3XD). Each zone demands a different action and profile.

KVD: In cold water cranking, use slow to medium retrieve. Start and stop often — any time during the retrieve, pausing can trigger a following bass into biting. Parallel the bank for long casts to keep the bait in the zone the entire cast.
KVD: Use lighter line in cold water cranking. Lighter line allows the bait to have its liveliest action and reach maximum depth. 10lb fluoro for the 3XD deep zone.

KVD: CC4 medium-heavy with a soft tip for cranking. 6.2:1 gear ratio reel (Paramag RX braking). The soft tip throws small cold-water crankbaits well and allows long casts for maximum time in the zone.

KVD: Bass in cold water really use their lateral line to hunt. Even in dirty water they can feel the pressure waves of a flatside bait. This is why flatsided crankbaits are 'magical' in cold water — the flat sides displace a lot of water.

KVD: Crawfish patterns dominate cold water. Clear water = natural/ghost crawfish (greens and browns). Stained = fire craw, brighter patterns like spring craw. If you see shad activity, shad patterns work too. Look at what color crawfish the fish are eating — is it green, brown, red, or purple? Match it.

KVD: In cold water, target transition areas where bass will stage before moving to spawning bays. Channel swing ends, flat edges, steeper banks transitioning to flatter areas, where different rock types come together. Chunk rock banks, bluffs, and riprap around bridges are prime for the mid-range crankbait.

KVD: 'Making sure your crankbait is contacting the bottom in any depth zone you're fishing — that is absolutely paramount.' The deflection off rocks, cover, even gravel makes a big difference in triggering cold water fish to bite.

KVD: Red Eye Shad sizes for depth control — 1/4oz for ultra-shallow (1-2ft), 3/8oz for shallow flats, 1/2oz as go-to, 3/4oz for 10-15ft deep drains. Plus tungsten Two Tap in 1/2 and 3/4oz — heavier tungsten fishes slightly deeper. Most anglers get stuck on 1/2oz and don't downsize when fish are shallow.

KVD: Line size is a major depth control tool for lipless crankbaits. Lighter line (10-12lb) = bait gets deeper (use for clear water, deeper grass, northern natural lakes). Heavier line (17-20lb) = bait stays higher and falls slower (use for shallow grass lakes like Guntersville). Rod tip up in shallow water.

KVD: CC3 medium-heavy composite (graphite/glass) cranking rod for lipless crankbaits. 6.2:1 gear ratio reel — not high-speed in cold water. The composite gives feel for detecting grass on hooks while having power to rip free.

KVD: Always change hooks to KVD/Mustad Triple Grip. Upsize: #2 on 1/2oz, #4 on 1/4oz, #1/0 on 3/4oz. Triple grip design locks fish on — critical because lipless crankbaits are known for poor strike-to-land ratio. Won the Classic catching 5-pounders hooked in the back and side thanks to these hooks.

KVD: Crawfish patterns dominate pre-spawn — reds in any water clarity (Rayburn Red, Delta Red, Fire Craw). Clear water = natural craw patterns (dark brown, baby carp, orange brim, bluegill). Stained/dirty = chartreuse, chartreuse sexy shad, brighter colors. Shad-focused systems (Tennessee River) = chrome sexy shad, gold sexy shad, straight sexy shad.

KVD: Fish the lipless crankbait like a jig in cold water. Short hop it along the bottom. Pull it around cover, deflect off rocks. Rip it out of grass, let it flutter down. Use rod tip to manipulate depth — tip up in shallow, down to get deeper. Make the bait fall as many times as possible — that's when bites happen.

KVD: Focus on large flats and spawning flats — where fish are coming and going. Look for deeper depressions, edges, drops, centers of drains within the flat. These subtle depth changes concentrate pre-spawn fish. At places like Toledo Bend and Sam Rayburn, fish deeper drains/ditches leading into spawning bays with 3/4oz.

KVD: At Toledo Bend, started each morning shallow on the shad spawn for 1-1.5 hours, then transitioned offshore to deep crank. The biggest fish all came deep cranking with 6XD, 8XD, 10XD. Didn't get a ton of bites but when he got bit, it was a giant.

KVD: 'Stop signs' = clean areas (hard bottom, rock transitions) that sit just outside major spawning coves. Fish stage on these as they move in and out of spawning areas. Concentrate on high-percentage corners. LakeMaster mapping showing bottom contours was the critical tool — 'the only reason I won.'
KVD: With a big lead, 30+ boats follow you. Spectators fish your spots after you leave. 'It's challenging when they're fishing your stuff.' One of the toughest aspects of being the frontrunner.

KVD: Cut the head off the blade minnow trailer to shorten it slightly. The water rushing around the chatterbait blade hits the shortened tail at the perfect distance for lively action. This is his #1 trailer for the Thunder Cricket.

KVD: 'Lakes that have grass change the most.' Creek channels don't move and bottom terrain is static, but grass lakes change a lot year to year — grass types, height, density all shift. Different areas of the same lake may have different grass types (hydrilla, eel grass, pepper grass, streaming grass).

KVD: In summer on grass lakes, big fish move offshore to the middle of the lake where the bait is. They don't stay near shore when it's hot. Look for scattered hydrilla clumps in the middle of the lake.
KVD: Heavy grass volume makes the water clearer. When grass clumps reach the surface, expect improved water clarity compared to areas without grass.

KVD: Choosing baits that are efficient for the type of cover in that depth zone is a big part of it. When grass is to the surface, a regular crankbait runs too deep — switch to a wake bait version to stay in the strike zone.
KVD: Stained water compensates for tough conditions like no wind and high bright skies — you can still trigger reaction bites. In clear water under those conditions, it would be much harder.

KVD: In cold water, you can't just cover water. The strike zone is very small — fish won't swim 10 feet to eat your bait. You have to get it right in front of them and 'knock them on the head.' Slow down and be more thorough.

KVD: 'You've got to fish the conditions and dictate your lure choice, your patterns, your style, your speed to those conditions — and as they change, you want to adjust.' This applies to any tournament or fishing day.

KVD: '45 degree water at Sam Rayburn Texas is a lot different than 45 degree water in Michigan.' Shallower natural lakes and lowland reservoirs with vegetation — fish don't go deep regardless of cold, they stay shallow. Highland reservoirs (Table Rock, Dale Hollow) — fish live deeper, suspend, roam following shad. Approach must match lake type.

KVD: When water temps drop into the 30s, threadfin shad start dying off and bass gorge. Can be the best fishing you've ever had. Signs: watch for birds (seagulls, herons) hovering over the middle of the lake on frosty mornings — birds tell you where the bait is, and that's where bass will be.

KVD: In clear deep lakes with shad kills, fish often suspend. The higher they are in the water column, the more active they are. Fish off main lake points, humps, drops, centers of big bays — not near the bank. Alabama rig with spinners (keeps rig up in water column) or minnow/swimbait on spotlight head are best for covering water around suspended fish.

KVD: In extreme cold, downsize everything. Clearer the water = smaller the bait. Coffee tube, Bitsy tube (Zona taught him this at ice-out in Michigan — 'absolute money'). Small compact minnow baits. Use just heavy enough weight to reach the zone.

KVD: 'The common misnomer is that bass slow down and don't bite in cold water. That is absolutely not true.' Growing up in Michigan, fish live under ice all winter and still feed actively. Cold water fishing is nothing to be intimidated by — it can be some of the best fishing of the season.

KVD: On lowland reservoirs with grass (Guntersville, Toledo Bend, Okeechobee), bass don't leave shallow areas even in extreme cold. They stage in slightly deeper areas adjacent to spawning flats — drains, ditches, depressions, holes in the grass. Old dead vegetation holds heat and draws bass. Fish caught in 1-2ft of water in upper 30s temps.

KVD: Bass in super cold water are focused on vibration. They use their lateral line to feed — they can feel the presence of shad, the vibration and water displacement of your bait. That's why flatside crankbaits, lipless crankbaits, and bladed jigs all work in extreme cold: vibration is the common thread.

KVD: Bill angle determines everything. Straighter bill = tighter action + steeper dive angle + deeper running. More angle from the body = wider wobble + more displacement + more vibration + quicker recovery after deflection. The line tie position relative to the bill matters too.

KVD: Flatside crankbaits send off a lot of pressure waves because flat sides displace a ton of water. Best in cold water (bass rely on lateral line to hunt) and dirty water any season (visibility is down). Round body crankbaits vary widely depending on bill angle — the body shape alone doesn't tell the whole story.

KVD: 'Pick a bait that runs just a little deeper than the depth of water you're fishing — that particular crankbait is going to be at its best.' It contacts bottom with great action. A bait that runs much deeper than the water overpowers the bottom and loses its triggering qualities.

KVD: The 10XD is unique for a straight-body deep diver — it has a slightly wider wobble than expected, giving it great deflection even in 12-15ft of water. Most deep divers with straight bills lose themselves completely when hitting cover. The 10XD doesn't — that's why it's a premier deep cranking bait.

KVD: Square bills are round-bodied with a steep angle and a square bill — this gives them a wide wobble and the best deflection qualities of any crankbait. Most weedless crankbait you can throw. Good through grass, brush, gnarly rocks, riprap. Works in cold or warm water. Triggers neutral/negative fish with deflection.

KVD: 'In 99% of circumstances, I want to make sure whatever depth I'm fishing, the bait is getting to the bottom.' Biggest mistake anglers make is using the wrong style crankbait and not contacting the bottom. That's why depth-specific models exist (10XD, 8XD, 6XD, 5XD, 3XD).

KVD: On clear water grass lakes, most anglers fish the inside line where fish spawn. But fish also spawn in the deeper scattered grass patches (7ft+) in the middle of big flats. Nobody else fishes it — that's a competitive advantage. A lot of largemouth have already spawned out there.

KVD: North wind blowing into a bank knocks algae loose and muddies the water, making sight fishing impossible. When this happens, switch from sight fishing to casting Thunder Cricket or fan casting the area. Conditions can clear up fast when wind lays down.

KVD: In northern natural lakes, perch and gobies are the primary forage — fish spend most of their time feeding on them. Both live on or near rocks. Birds diving on shallow points = alewives spawning (another forage clue).
KVD: 'So important in clear water lakes that you've got good polarized glasses.' They let you see green grass clumps further out so you can pitch to them without getting right on top of spooky fish.

KVD: In practice, keep moving and take notes. Don't fall in love with one area. Stop at many stretches, get a feel for what's there, then move on. 'That's why we call it practice.' Save the commitment for tournament day.

KVD: In clear water slick calm conditions, most anglers reach for finesse. KVD's instinct is to jerk a jerkbait over the top of them — the clear water means they'll see it from a distance. Confidence in the technique matters as much as conditions.

KVD: During pre-spawn, when you find one fish on a spot, they're usually grouped. Got a bite every single cast on one little spot on a point. They stage together before moving up to spawn. Mark the spot and work it thoroughly.

KVD: Clear water on grass lakes = fish can see the bait from farther away (benefit) but can also be spooky (challenge). Fish may be timid — bites are subtle, just bumping the bait. Slow down and keep working the area.

KVD: Bite the head off the KVD Magic trailer — shorten it just a bit. Combined with blue craw Thunder Cricket, it looks like a bluegill. The trailer adds a little flash and keeps the presentation subtle and natural for clear water.
KVD: CC5 composite rod with heavy action (e-glass/graphite blend) — you feel everything with the bladed jig and it absorbs shock on the bite. 7.5:1 KVD reel, 20lb fluorocarbon.

KVD: On Cayuga Lake (northern natural lake), the north end is a huge expanse of flats at 8-12ft, all grass. The key is finding clean holes in the grass at 9-11ft. Good holes have a little bit of rock in them. Each hole has its own ecosystem — bluegill, perch, gobies all live there. Good holes have reloaded with fish all week.

KVD: For pitching into grass holes — 3/8oz tungsten weight with a 3/0 Mustad grip pin hook (the pin keeps the bait from sliding down). 5-inch Ocho worm. Blue craw or green pumpkin. Making it fall straight is critical — the Texas rig glides down clean into the holes.

KVD: Position boat just outside the grass line. Cast at 45° quartering angle into shallower grass. Crank down until you hit grass, then slow up. If it won't float free, snap it out. The bait follows the contour of the grass as it comes back to the deeper edge where your boat sits. This is the most effective way to cover a lot of grass quickly.

KVD: When crankbait sticks in grass — if it won't come through, snap it free. Otherwise let it float off. The moment it tears free or floats off is when you get the bite. 'A crankbait through the grass is almost as good as a finesse bait like a worm at triggering strikes from inactive fish.'

KVD: Fluorocarbon is mandatory for cranking grass — low stretch helps rip bait free. Soft-tip composite rod (glass/graphite blend) for feel and power. Multiple rods with different line sizes. Series 5 crankbait runs 10-12ft — wants a bait that runs to the edge of the grass, not one that buries into it.

KVD: The crankbait is a search tool for reading the grass line — you can feel where grass comes out farther, where it's taller, subtle points and cuts. Fish relate to irregularities in the grass edge. On natural lakes where structure is subtle, the crankbait finds what your eyes can't.

KVD: Guntersville's grass has evolved over the years: milfoil first, then hydrilla, now a lot of eel grass. All three still present. The grass creates great habitat and clear water (filters it). Floating eel grass cut up by wind/waves/boats makes it hard to throw lipless crankbaits and jerkbaits — catches on your line.

KVD: Guntersville breaks into three sections: Lower end = rocks, bluffs, bridges (good for jerkbaits, cranking). Mid-lake = river ledges, miles of grass (always a player). Upper end = shallow creeks, lily pads (Bill Lowen territory — guys who get way back into places others don't look).

KVD: 'One of the things when I'm preparing for a big tournament is I really want to follow the weather — not only current conditions but what's been happening leading up to the event.' When lakes are off-limits for a month+, weather tracking is how you prepare. Water clarity, temp trends, rain history all matter.

KVD: Mild winters at Guntersville keep water in low 50s with grass still green and water exceptionally clear. Cold winters push shad into creek centers with wolf packs of bass around them. Both scenarios produce but require completely different approaches.

KVD: Three weight tricks for Neko rigs: (1) Superglue all tungsten Neko weights — they won't fly out when fish jump. (2) Wrap lead wire from fly tying department (Bass Pro) around the hook shank for subtle extra weight without bulk. (3) Put weights in both ends of creature baits like the Sexy Bug — hook in the middle, weight in both nose and tail.

KVD: GS1 spinning rod, 6'10" medium action for accuracy around docks. Longer spinning rod for open water distance. 8lb braid to 8lb fluoro leader. Mustad Apex weedless hook for cover/docks, exposed hook in open water. Mustad Apex drop shot hook (wide gap) for creature baits.

KVD: Dice/bug baits on a drop shot are a new frontier. Lighter weight = better (1/8oz ideal, 1/4oz max in deeper water). The slow sinking action is the key — once on bottom you can still shake it traditionally, but most bites come on the fall. Game-changer for clear water smallmouth and during Mayfly hatches.

KVD: Neko-rigged creature baits are 'deadly springtime bait around the spawn.' In clear water, most fish bite on the initial fall — if they're on a bed, the bait lands next to them and they eat it immediately. Works for both largemouth and smallmouth/spots.

KVD: Match the forage and water clarity. Spring in Texas = red (crawfish coming out, dark red). Stained water = brighter reds like fire craw or chili craw. Gold has great flash in stained water (gold sexy shad). General rule: 'Pick something that's natural and something that's visible.' Match bluegill, crawfish, or shad based on the system.

KVD: As water warms in pre-spawn, fish scatter out into big spawning bays. Try to follow ditches but sometimes you just have to fan cast randomly. Wind or clouds help position fish better and make them more aggressive.

KVD: Day 1 of practice = get a big picture view of the whole lake. Day 2 = dial in and refine. Find areas and patterns that fit your strengths. Once you find the best area, everything else is just a secondary pattern.

KVD: 'You got to find areas and patterns that fit your strengths.' As a power fisherman, KVD looks for areas where power techniques (crankbaits, bladed jigs) can produce quality bites, not just numbers.
KVD: 7'2" medium light with extra fast tip (GS4 LSE KVD rod). Uses braid for maximum feel — 'with that braid, you can feel everything.' Bites can be very subtle.

KVD: Don't set the hook traditionally on a Ned Rig. Reel into them fast — let the rod load, then pull. If you miss one, immediately drop it right back down because they'll come right back and bite it.

KVD: The Ned Rig is so popular for smallmouth because it imitates gobies. Gobies stay locked on the bottom on rocks — that's exactly where the Ned Rig sits. Find the rocks, you find the big smallmouth. Gobies have changed these fisheries — fish don't have to move because the food source is so prolific.
KVD: Use the lightest Ned head you can get away with. 1/16 oz in shallow, 3/16 oz in deep water or heavy wind. Lighter head = better action on the bait.

KVD: When fishing the Ned Rig on an edge, the main thing is keeping yourself positioned on the edge where the fish are. Spot Lock (trolling motor GPS anchor) is essential — 'without that spot lock, I would never be able to stay positioned or hold or land one.'

KVD: For lipless crankbaits in shallow grass (3-7ft): CC5 7'4" heavy action composite rod, 7.5:1 reel, 20lb fluoro. Multiple cranking rods with 14, 17, and 20lb line for different depths. Heavy line keeps the bait up and slows it down in shallow grass. The heavy composite rod has backbone to rip through grass.

KVD: Pre-spawn in Texas (and everywhere), follow the ditches and drains leading into spawning flats. Fish use these as highways. Subtle depressions on LakeMaster mapping concentrate fish, but sometimes the best spots are subtle features you find just by covering water with a Red Eye Shad.
KVD: Texas pre-spawn isn't just about Rayburn and Toledo Bend. Many other Texas lakes have big fish. Pre-spawn in Texas = follow ditches and drains leading to spawning flats.

KVD: 'I don't spend more than 10 minutes in the same area doing the same thing without making some kind of change.' Either move, change baits, change presentation — but don't waste time without positive feedback. Any sign (blowup, follow, catch, short bite) is a clue to the puzzle.

KVD: Most important thing arriving at a lake: figure out the seasonal pattern plus current conditions. Even on a known lake, see what's changed. On grass lakes, determine how much grass there is and where. Look for bottlenecks and funnels on your mapping. Cover a lot of water on day 1.

KVD: On grass lakes, look for bottlenecks and funnels on your LakeMaster mapping — cuts and narrows between grass mats. Areas leading in and out of these bottlenecks can be really good, especially with wind creating current.

KVD: Keeps 3/8, 1/2, and 5/8 oz Thunder Crickets on the deck during pre-spawn. Uses lighter weight (3/8oz) to slow down in tough conditions. Tungsten Thunder Cricket has smaller blade with tighter/subtler vibration and runs deeper in the same size — better for finicky fish.

KVD: In cold/post-frontal pre-spawn, don't just wind the Thunder Cricket. Work it like a jig — hop, twitch, pull-and-stop. Get it down in the grass and on the bottom. Bites are extremely subtle: the bait just gets heavy or the vibration stops. 'I always move faster than the fish' — force yourself to slow down.

KVD: 7'4" medium-heavy rod (KVD Elite GC7), 7.5:1 gear ratio reel, 20lb fluorocarbon. Heavy line does NOT affect spinnerbait action — it's one of the few lures where heavy line doesn't hurt. Allows accurate casts tight to cover.

KVD: Barely turn the handle. When the bait hits a limb, speed the reel up one fast turn to force the bait over the limb keeping it up and down (not turning over and hanging). The flutter on the other side of the limb is when they bite. Even without cover, intermittently speed up for a quick flare of the skirt and blades to trigger following fish.

KVD: Always use a plastic trailer when slow rolling — adds bulk and extra action fish home in on. Rage Slim Swimmer is his go-to: subtle, not too bulky (doesn't make bait ride up), has an erratic wag. In coldest months, sometimes removes the skirt entirely and just uses a swimbait as trailer.

KVD: Bright sunny + clear water = natural colors (bluegill, spot remover). Dirty water = brightest or darkest colors (chartreuse/white or black/blue) to maximize silhouette. Shad patterns (white/chartreuse) are day-in day-out staples. Spring around grass in Texas = red crawfish patterns. Match the forage and water clarity.

KVD: When slow rolling, keep the bait ABOVE the bass's line of sight — you're imitating baitfish, not crawfish. Fish cover or fish over the bass's head. Steep rocky banks, laydowns, grass — the bait should be slowly ticking the cover above where bass are positioned.
KVD: Spinnerbait is one of the few lures where heavy line (20lb fluoro) does NOT hinder the action at all. On a small crankbait or finesse jig, heavy line hurts the action — but not on a spinnerbait.

KVD: Red Eye Shad sizes for depth control — 1/4oz for ultra-shallow (1-2ft), 3/8oz for shallow flats, 1/2oz as go-to, 3/4oz for 10-15ft deep drains. Plus tungsten Two Tap in 1/2 and 3/4oz — heavier tungsten fishes slightly deeper. Most anglers get stuck on 1/2oz and don't downsize when fish are shallow.

Heavier line (17-20lb) = bait stays higher and falls slower (use for shallow grass lakes like Guntersville). Rod tip up in shallow water.

Triple grip design locks fish on — critical because lipless crankbaits are known for poor strike-to-land ratio. Won the Classic catching 5-pounders hooked in the back and side thanks to these hooks.

These subtle depth changes concentrate pre-spawn fish. At places like Toledo Bend and Sam Rayburn, fish deeper drains/ditches leading into spawning bays with 3/4oz.
KVD: 'Spring = crawfish patterns with red. Summer = shad and bluegill patterns. Fall = shad patterns in creek backs. When the 1.5 hits grass, clear hooks with a hard snap of the rod tip.'

KVD: 'Simple rule: lighter spinnerbait with smaller blades in shallow water, larger bait in deep water. The KVD Finesse Spinnerbait (3/8oz, one willow + smaller Colorado) is a great all-around option for weeds, stumps, docks.'

KVD: 'Offshore spinnerbait: 3/4oz, trim wire down a third, single willow blade. Cast, let helicopter down to bottom on tight line. Rip up sharply, let flutter back down. Provokes reaction strikes from suspended bass in summer heat.'

KVD: 'Tandem willow blades for horizontal retrieves through grass — less lift, runs deeper. Single blade for vertical presentations and helicopter flutter. Colorado/willow combo for slower retrieves and longer time in strike zone.'

KVD: 'Position boat to fish parallel to edges and cover. Keeps the bait in the strike zone longer. Short accurate casts (30-40ft) for control in dense cover. Soft underhand presentations so bait lands quietly in shallow water.'

KVD: At Toledo Bend, started each morning shallow on the shad spawn for 1-1.5 hours, then transitioned offshore to deep crank. The biggest fish all came deep cranking with 6XD, 8XD, 10XD. Didn't get a ton of bites but when he got bit, it was a giant.

Concentrate on high-percentage corners. LakeMaster mapping showing bottom contours was the critical tool — 'the only reason I won.'
KVD: With a big lead, 30+ boats follow you. Spectators fish your spots after you leave. 'It's challenging when they're fishing your stuff.' One of the toughest aspects of being the frontrunner.

KVD: 'Grassy lakes have 3 depth zones: Shallow flats (0-4ft) — topwater, spinnerbaits, weedless plastics for sight-feeding fish. Weed edges (4-8ft) — the sweet spot, bass ambush from the edge, use soft plastics, jigs, creature baits. Deeper transitions (8ft+) — where grass tops out, fish suspend, use deep cranks, jigging spoons, blade baits. Cast along features, not straight across flats.'

KVD: 'Reading grassy lake water: color breaks (darker water = deeper edges), grass clumps (ambush points), wind-driven current seams (concentrate bass). Match bait to time of day AND depth zone: morning/evening = topwater/shallow, midday = soft plastics/spinnerbaits on weed edges, late/low light = crankbaits and deeper jigs.'

KVD: Cut the head off the blade minnow trailer to shorten it slightly. The water rushing around the chatterbait blade hits the shortened tail at the perfect distance for lively action. This is his #1 trailer for the Thunder Cricket.
